evidence.title: Management of Sore Throat: Time to Update | AAFP
evidence.snippet: Because rheumatic heart disease is so rare in the United States, the primary benefit of using antibiotics is symptom reduction, with a number needed to treat of 6 for improvement by day 3, and possibly the reduction of spread to others (although most close contacts have likely already been exposed by the time the patient is evaluated). 15 , 16 In 2023, an international group of experts in primary care respiratory infections published the first international guideline for the management of sore throat . 3 The guideline focuses on avoiding testing and antibiotic use for patients with uncomplicated sore throat (i.e., mild to moderate sore throat accompanied by cough with no red flags), reserving evaluation, testing, and possibly antibiotic use for those at risk of complicated or severe disease. 3 Using rapid antigen testing for streptococcus, followed by prescribing antibiotics, is not reco

evidence.title: Recommendations | Sore throat (acute): antimicrobial prescribing | Guidance | NICE
evidence.snippet: 250 mg to 500 mg four times a day or 500 mg to 1,000 mg twice a day for 5 days Erythromycin is preferred if a macrolide is needed in pregnancy, for example, if there is true penicillin allergy and the benefits of antibiotic treatment outweigh the harms.

note_text:
S: The patient is a 47-year-old female presenting with a sore throat of 2 days duration accompanied by fever, chills, swollen lymph nodes, and white patches on the tonsils. She denies cough, nasal congestion, difficulty swallowing, rash, difficulty breathing, or drooling. She has no prior history of strep throat and has tried symptomatic treatment with tea, honey, and DayQuil without improvement.

O: Phone encounter only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: The working diagnosis is acute bacterial pharyngitis, likely streptococcal given the presence of fever, tonsillar exudates, and swollen lymph nodes without cough.
Differential:
1. Streptococcal pharyngitis
2. Viral pharyngitis
3. Infectious mononucleosis
4. Other bacterial pharyngitis (e.g., group C or G streptococcus)

P: - First-line medication: Azithromycin 500 mg orally on day 1, then 250 mg orally daily on days 2 through 5 (Physician must verify medication/dosing)
- Alternatives: Clindamycin 300 mg orally three times daily for 10 days (Physician must verify medication/dosing)
- Medication safety considerations: Patient is allergic to penicillin; avoid penicillin and related beta-lactams. Monitor for gastrointestinal side effects with macrolides and clindamycin. Other contraindications not assessed in this telephone encounter.
- Follow-up recommendations: Seek in-person medical care urgently if difficulty breathing, drooling, severe pain, inability to swallow, or worsening symptoms occur. Seek emergency care if airway obstruction or severe systemic symptoms develop.
- Testing: Arrange for rapid antigen detection test or throat culture for group A streptococcus if possible to confirm diagnosis and guide therapy.
